overview.summary: Cuba's rich Afro-Cuban culture has profoundly influenced music, dance, and spiritual practices worldwide. With a significant population of African descent, Cuba has created unique cultural expressions that blend Yoruba, Congo, and other African traditions with Spanish colonial influences.
overview.historicalContext: Between 1511 and 1886, Cuba received nearly one million enslaved Africans, making it one of the last countries in the Americas to abolish slavery. The island's sugar plantation economy created conditions where African cultural practices could be preserved and transformed into distinctly Cuban expressions.

keyFacts: One of the last countries to abolish slavery (1886)
Birthplace of salsa, mambo, and rumba music
Strong preservation of Yoruba religious traditions (Santería)
Major influence on Latin American and world music

music.name: Rumba
music.description: Traditional Afro-Cuban music and dance complex featuring drums, vocals, and expressive movement.
music.africanRoots: Based on Central African and Yoruba rhythmic patterns and call-and-response vocals
music.significance: Represents pure African musical expression adapted to Cuban urban environment

music.name: Salsa
music.description: Popular dance music that evolved from Cuban son and other Afro-Cuban rhythms.
music.africanRoots: Built on African polyrhythmic foundation with clave rhythm as organizing principle
music.significance: Spread Afro-Cuban musical concepts throughout Latin America and the world

music.name: Santería Music
music.description: Sacred music used in Yoruba-derived religious ceremonies and rituals.
music.africanRoots: Direct continuation of Yoruba religious music and ceremonial drumming
music.significance: Maintains African spiritual music traditions and religious practices

music.name: Conga
music.description: Street carnival music featuring large drums and participatory dancing.
music.africanRoots: Derives from Central African processional music and community celebrations
music.significance: Preserves African communal music-making and festival traditions

food.name: Moros y Cristianos
food.description: Black beans and rice dish that represents the fusion of African and Spanish culinary traditions.
food.africanRoots: Bean preparation methods and one-pot cooking techniques from West Africa
food.significance: Symbolizes cultural fusion while maintaining African nutritional wisdom

food.name: Tostones
food.description: Twice-fried plantains served as a popular side dish throughout Cuba.
food.africanRoots: Plantain preparation techniques brought from West and Central Africa
food.significance: Demonstrates African agricultural knowledge and cooking methods

food.name: Yuca con Mojo
food.description: Cassava root served with garlic and citrus sauce.
food.africanRoots: Cassava processing and preparation methods from African culinary traditions
food.significance: Maintains African knowledge of root vegetable preparation and nutrition

religion.name: Santería (Regla de Ocha)
religion.description: Syncretic religion that preserves Yoruba spiritual practices within Catholic framework.
religion.africanRoots: Direct continuation of Yoruba religious system with orisha worship and divination
religion.significance: Maintains African spiritual worldview and religious practices in the Americas

religion.name: Palo Monte
religion.description: Afro-Cuban religion based on Central African (Congo) spiritual traditions.
religion.africanRoots: Preserves Bantu religious practices including ancestor worship and natural spirits
religion.significance: Maintains Central African spiritual traditions and healing practices

religion.name: Abakuá
religion.description: Secret society based on West African (Calabar) fraternal organizations.
religion.africanRoots: Direct continuation of Efik and Ejagham secret society traditions from Nigeria/Cameroon
religion.significance: Preserves African social organization and masculine spiritual traditions

language.name: Afro-Cuban Spanish
language.description: Cuban Spanish enriched with vocabulary and expressions from African languages.
language.africanRoots: Incorporates words and concepts from Yoruba, Kikongo, and other African languages
language.significance: Demonstrates African linguistic influence on Cuban Spanish

language.name: Lucumí Language
language.description: Liturgical language used in Santería ceremonies, derived from Yoruba.
language.africanRoots: Direct preservation of Yoruba language for religious and ceremonial purposes
language.significance: Maintains African sacred language and religious terminology

festivals.name: Carnival
festivals.description: Annual celebration featuring Afro-Cuban music, dance, and cultural expressions.
festivals.africanRoots: Incorporates African masquerade traditions and community celebration patterns
festivals.significance: Showcases Afro-Cuban culture and maintains African festival traditions

festivals.name: Día de San Lázaro
festivals.description: Religious festival honoring Saint Lazarus (syncretized with Yoruba orisha Babalú-Ayé).
festivals.africanRoots: Combines Catholic saint veneration with Yoruba orisha worship traditions
festivals.significance: Demonstrates African religious syncretism and spiritual continuity

featuredImage.alt: Cuban salsa dancers performing in traditional colorful costumes
featuredImage.caption: Cuban salsa and rumba carry African rhythms and expressions to the world

gallery.alt: Santería ceremony with traditional white clothing and offerings
gallery.caption: Santería preserves Yoruba spiritual traditions within Cuban culture

gallery.alt: Traditional Cuban batá drums used in Santería ceremonies
gallery.caption: Batá drums maintain the sacred rhythms of Yoruba religious traditions

gallery.alt: Cuban carnival celebration with elaborate costumes and masks
gallery.caption: Cuban carnival showcases the vibrant fusion of African and Caribbean cultures
